ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pig liver food product class; no specific stir-fried pig liver class exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers cooked pig liver; no stir-fried-specific code exists. Closest parent match used for the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried pig liver as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried pig liver is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small plate portion as commonly served in a Singaporean zi char or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Pig Liver — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Pig Liver (food code F06100045). Core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced exactly. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and micronutrient values not reported in HPB are set to -1 or estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork liver cooked (NDB 10717) as a secondary reference for supplementary fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried pig liver. Pig liver is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food (2.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g). GI is estimated at approximately 32 by analogy with other cooked organ meats and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 2.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.1, classified as very low. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat matrix slowing any glucose response from the minimal carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg per 100 g and very high cholesterol (~260 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced soy sauce in preparation to keep sodium below 300 mg per 100 g, and avoid versions prepared with Shaoxing wine as alcohol raises blood pressure; the high cholesterol content also warrants limiting frequency to once per week.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.3 g per 100 g) and high protein (13.8 g per 100 g) support glycaemic stability, but very high cholesterol (~260 mg per 100 g) is a concern for diabetes-related dyslipidaemia
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried pig liver has a low glycaemic load and high protein content that supports glycaemic stability in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the very high cholesterol content (~260 mg per 100 g) means portion size should be limited to 100–120 g and frequency restricted to once per week to manage cardiovascular risk associated with diabetes-related dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high dietary cholesterol (~260 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium (314 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat stir-fried pig liver as an occasional food only (no more than once per fortnight), limit portions to 80–100 g, request low-sodium preparation, and avoid versions with added Shaoxing wine or excess oyster sauce, given the high cholesterol and sodium burden that can adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried pig liver (炒猪肝, Chǎo Zhū Gān) is a classic Singaporean Chinese zi char and home-cooked dish consisting of thinly sliced pig liver rapidly stir-fried in a hot wok with aromatics including onion, ginger, and spring onion, typically seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The dish is prized for its tender yet slightly firm texture, rich umami flavour, and characteristic iron-forward taste of liver. Pig liver is one of the most nutrient-dense organ meats available, providing 13.8 g of protein, 4.7 mg of iron, and 20 mg of calcium per 100 g, alongside 164 kcal of energy. The fat content is 11.1 g per 100 g, reflecting the cooking oil used in stir-frying. Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.3 g per 100 g, giving the dish a low glycaemic index and glycaemic load, making it broadly compatible with low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, pig liver is very high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 260 mg per 100 g), which is a significant clinical consideration for individuals with cardiovascular disease, diabetes-related dyslipidaemia, or hypercholesterolaemia. Sodium is moderate at 314 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce and oyster sauce used in seasoning. The dish is not suitable for halal diets as it contains pork. It is dairy-free and gluten-free only if tamari is substituted for soy sauce. Clinically, the dish is best consumed in controlled portions of 100–150 g and limited in frequency for those managing cardiovascular risk, hypertension, or Type 2 Diabetes with dyslipidaemia.
common_user_queries: How much cholesterol is in pig liver?
Can diabetics eat stir-fried pig liver?
Is pig liver good for iron deficiency?
Is stir-fried pig liver high in sodium?
Can hypertension patients eat pig liver?

confidence_reason: Core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are sourced exactly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Pig Liver (food code F06100045), which is a canonical approved primary source for Singaporean dishes. Confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried pig liver preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06100045, and no sufficiently close match in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the composite dish as prepared provides these values with confidence. Saturated fat (2.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (260 mg per 100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork liver cooked data (NDB 10717) as a secondary reference, adjusted for the stir-frying preparation context; these are documented as estimated values. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for high-protein, very low-carbohydrate cooked organ meats. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. Dietary tag 'keto' from input is retained as the carbohydrate content (2.3 g per 100 g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
